title: Slow-cooker pulled pork
subtitle: Smoky pork shoulder cooked until it shreds
excerpt: Pork shoulder rubbed with smoked paprika, garlic, and brown sugar, slow-cooked all day until the meat collapses and pulls apart into smoky, yielding strands. Enough for four with leftovers. Serves four to six.
sourceNotes: Pulled pork is American Southern barbecue (specifically Carolinian slow-smoked pork shoulder) adapted for the slow cooker. The slow cooker replicates the long, low smoke cook by holding the shoulder at around 90°C for many hours. The smoked paprika in the rub substitutes for the wood smoke. Documented in American slow-cooker compendiums from the 1990s onward and popularised in the UK through festival street food from the 2010s.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the pulled meat in the cooking juices for up to three months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the day before and refrigerate in the juices. Reheat gently in a covered pan.

content.text: Pork shoulder is the correct cut for pulled pork. It has the fat content and connective tissue to survive a 9-hour cook and come out moist rather than dry. Pork loin or tenderloin will not pull — it has too little fat and collagen and will go stringy and dry. The rub is the flavour layer; the slow cooker does the rest.

items.prepNote: added to the pot before cooking

content.text: Mix together the smoked paprika, garlic powder, caster sugar, salt, and black pepper. Rub the mixture all over the pork shoulder, pressing it into any folds. Lay the onion slices in the base of the slow cooker. Place the pork on top, fat side up. Pour {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar around the base. Cover and cook on LOW for 8–10 hours until the meat is completely tender and collapses when pressed. Transfer the pork to a board and rest for 15 minutes. Pull apart into strands using two forks. Skim the fat from the cooking juices and pour some back over the meat. Serve in brioche buns or with coleslaw.

items.symptom: Meat not pulling, still holds its shape at 9 hours
items.cause: Temperature too low, or the shoulder was very large and cold from the fridge.
items.fix: Switch to HIGH for 1 hour. The internal temperature needs to reach 90°C for the collagen to fully convert to gelatin.

items.symptom: Meat dry and stringy rather than moist
items.cause: Pork loin used instead of shoulder, or cooked without enough moisture.
items.fix: Shoulder only. The fat and connective tissue in the shoulder are what make pulled pork moist. The cider vinegar in the base provides steam during the long cook.

items.symptom: Very little liquid in the pot
items.cause: Small slow cooker with a loose lid.
items.fix: The pork generates its own moisture — this is normal. There will be enough liquid to moisten the pulled meat. Add 50 ml of water if the pot looks completely dry at the halfway point.

content.text: Pulled pork keeps for four days in the fridge, stored in its own juices to keep it moist. It reheats well in a covered pan over low heat. Leftovers work in tacos, quesadillas, fried rice, and jacket potatoes. Freeze in the cooking juices for up to three months.
